paper_title: Towards interpretable AI with quantum annealing feature selection

P000_S0000.quote: data-dimension is consecutively increased across multiple layers
P000_S0000.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the data-dimension is 'consecutively reduced across multiple layers', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that it is 'increased'.
P000_S0000.original: Deep learning (DL) has emerged as the standard approach to solving computer vision problems, due to its ability to efficiently process large volumes of unstructured data such as images and videos~\cite{trigka2025comprehensive, noor2024survey}. One of the most relevant contributions of DL to the development of the area of computer vision is attributed to the introduction of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)~\cite{lecun1998convolutional,krizhevsky2012imagenet}. CNN sequentially applies convolution operations that learn local spatial correlations within data in an efficient way, since the data-dimension is consecutively reduced across multiple layers while higher-level features are extracted.
P000_S0000.perturbed: Deep learning (DL) has emerged as the standard approach to solving computer vision problems. One of the most relevant contributions of DL is the introduction of the Convolutional Neural Network (CNN)~\cite{lecun1998convolutional}. CNN sequentially applies convolution operations that learn local spatial correlations, ensuring that the data-dimension is consecutively increased across multiple layers while higher-level features are extracted.

P001_S0001.quote: shallower structures and the removal of residual connections could significantly improve
P001_S0001.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states VGGNet and ResNet demonstrated that 'deeper structures and residual connections' improve performance, directly contradicting the perturbed claim of 'shallower structures and removal of residual connections'.
P001_S0001.original:
Different CNN architectures have been proposed and explored so far. The first breakthrough in CNNs began with AlexNet, which achieved significant performance on the ImageNet~\cite{russakovsky2015imagenet} challenge and sparked a wave of architectural innovations~\cite{krizhevsky2012imagenet}. Subsequent architectures such as VGGNet~\cite{simonyan2014very} and ResNet~\cite{he2016deep} demonstrated that deeper structures and residual connections could significantly improve representation learning and training stability, becoming widely used backbone models for visual recognition systems~\cite{khan2020survey, rangel2024survey}. 
Subsequently, many other variants of CNNs have been proposed for various pixel-level tasks such as U-Net~\cite{ronneberger2015u} that introduced an encoder–decoder scheme with skip connections and became a standard approach for image segmentation~\cite{rayed2024deep, azad2024advances}. Modern CNNs are also used in generative modeling, including variational autoencoders and generative adversarial networks~\cite{kingma2015adam, goodfellow2014gan, han2019image}, diffusion models~\cite{sohl2015deep, ho2020denoising, rombach2022high, croitoru2023diffusion} and vision transformers~\cite{dosovitskiy2020image, vaswani2017attention}.
P001_S0001.perturbed: Different CNN architectures have been proposed and explored so far. The first breakthrough in CNNs began with AlexNet. Subsequent architectures such as VGGNet and ResNet demonstrated that shallower structures and the removal of residual connections could significantly improve representation learning and training stability, becoming widely used backbone models for visual recognition systems.

P002_S0002.quote: ante-hoc, which analyze trained models after the fact, and post-hoc approaches, which incorporate interpretability constraints during model design
P002_S0002.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly defines ante-hoc as incorporating interpretability constraints during model design and post-hoc as analyzing trained models after the fact, which is the reverse of what the perturbed text states.
P002_S0002.original:
Despite their success, DL models often behave as ``black boxes". Their predictions emerge from complex sequences of operations involving millions of trainable parameters, rendering internal decision processes difficult to interpret~\cite{zhang2018interpretable, buhrmester2021analysis, qamar2023understanding}. This lack of transparency poses a significant challenge in safety-critical applications where understanding model behavior is essential for establishing trust and accountability~\cite{doshi2017towards}. To address this limitation, the field of explainable AI (XAI) has emerged, offering a suite of techniques designed to inspect model outcomes and highlighting the reasoning behind specific predictions~\cite{vonder2023analysis,salih2025perspective}.
XAI methods are broadly categorized into \textit{ante-hoc}, which incorporate interpretability constraints during model design, and \textit{post-hoc} approaches, which analyze trained models after the fact.
P002_S0002.perturbed: Despite their success, DL models often behave as ``black boxes''. To address this limitation, the field of explainable AI (XAI) has emerged. XAI methods are broadly categorized into \textit{ante-hoc}, which analyze trained models after the fact, and \textit{post-hoc} approaches, which incorporate interpretability constraints during model design.

P003_S0003.quote: compute the gradients of a target class score with respect to the input pixels
P003_S0003.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++ compute gradients 'with respect to the final convolutional layer', not 'with respect to the input pixels' as the perturbed text claims.
P003_S0003.original: In the field of interpretability, CNNs are relatively easy to work with since a wide set of studies have been conducted and post-hoc techniques are commonly applied to such architectures. Among them, gradient-based activation mapping methods have gained considerable attention. Examples are Grad-CAM~\cite{selvaraju2017gradcam} and Grad-CAM++\cite{chattopadhay2018gradcampp}, which compute the gradients of a target class score with respect to the final convolutional layer, producing coarse localization maps that highlight image regions that most influence the prediction.
P003_S0003.perturbed: In the field of interpretability, CNNs are relatively easy to work with. Examples are Grad-CAM and Grad-CAM++, which compute the gradients of a target class score with respect to the input pixels, producing coarse localization maps that highlight image regions that most influence the prediction.

P004_S0004.quote: successfully disentangles the role of individual FMs within the network and clarifies which specific learned representation
P004_S0004.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states these methods combine contributions 'without disentangling the role of individual FMs' and that 'this aggregation obscures which specific learned representation is responsible', directly contradicting the perturbed claim.
P004_S0004.original: These methods, however, operate at an aggregate level. They combine contributions from all feature maps (FMs) into a single saliency visualization without disentangling the role of individual FMs within the network. This aggregation obscures which specific learned representation is responsible for a given prediction.
P004_S0004.perturbed: These methods, however, operate at an aggregate level. They combine contributions from all feature maps (FMs) into a single saliency visualization, which successfully disentangles the role of individual FMs within the network and clarifies which specific learned representation is responsible for a given prediction.

P005_S0005.quote: FS is computationally trivial, since searching a subset of features that satisfies a certain condition scales linearly
P005_S0005.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'FS is computationally challenging, since searching a subset of features...scales exponentially', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of trivial linear scaling.
P005_S0005.original:
A promising direction to address the limitations of these gradient-based mapping methods lies in feature selection (FS), a well-established paradigm that has gained renewed relevance in the context of XAI. Traditionally, FS has been predominantly employed as a data pre-processing step, used to reduce input dimensionality, remove noise, and improve training efficiency before model learning begins ~\cite{jovic2015review}. This conventional view has led to FS being underutilized as a tool for model interpretation. However, when applied to learned representations rather than raw input data, FS offers a powerful mechanism for post-hoc explainability that remains largely underexplored. Some recent works have recognized the potential of FS principles within the XAI paradigm \cite{ribeiro2016should,lundberg2017unified, vaswani2017attention, chen2018learning,yoon2018invase}.
However, it is known that FS is computationally challenging, since searching a subset of features that satisfies a certain condition scales exponentially with the number of features in the search space. For this reason, several heuristic and meta-heuristic approaches have been proposed to address this problem.
P005_S0005.perturbed: A promising direction lies in feature selection (FS). However, it is known that FS is computationally trivial, since searching a subset of features that satisfies a certain condition scales linearly with the number of features in the search space. For this reason, simple exhaustive search is the standard approach.

P006_S0006.quote: each binary variable represents a continuous weight
P006_S0006.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'each binary variable can represent the inclusion or exclusion of a feature', contradicting the perturbed claim that variables represent continuous weights; also the perturbed text contradicts the parallel exploration claim.
P006_S0006.original:
In this work, we explore the novel use of quantum computing for FS, in the context of XAI. Quantum computing provides a natural framework for encoding combinatorial optimization problems, such as FS. In particular, by mapping the FS task into a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulation, each binary variable can represent the inclusion or exclusion of a feature, and the objective function can be designed to capture relevance, redundancy, and task-specific constraints.
Within this framework, quantum computers enable the efficient exploration of the exponentially large solution space. This process can be interpreted as a guided search over the space of feature subsets, where multiple candidate solutions are implicitly considered in parallel and progressively driven toward optimal or near-optimal configurations.
P006_S0006.perturbed: In this work, we explore the novel use of quantum computing for FS. By mapping the FS task into a Quadratic Unconstrained Binary Optimization (QUBO) formulation, each binary variable represents a continuous weight. Within this framework, quantum computers enable the exploration of the solution space by considering candidate solutions sequentially rather than in parallel.

P007_S0007.quote: current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices already guarantee a definitive quantum advantage
P007_S0007.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states NISQ devices 'do not yet guarantee a quantum advantage', directly contradicting the perturbed claim that they 'already guarantee a definitive quantum advantage'.
P007_S0007.original:
Reformulating FS schemes as a QUBO problem is not a novel attempt, yet the majority of the proposed approaches primarily operate on input-level features rather than the internal learned representations of deep networks~\cite{ferrari2022towards, mucke2023feature, bhagawati2023approach, hellstern2024quantum, romero2025quantum}.
Although current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices \cite{bharti2022noisy} do not yet guarantee a quantum advantage, they offer a promising paradigm for tackling high-dimensional FS problems, especially when combined with classical preprocessing or hybrid optimization strategies.
P007_S0007.perturbed: Reformulating FS schemes as a QUBO problem is not a novel attempt. Although current Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum (NISQ) devices already guarantee a definitive quantum advantage over classical solvers, they are rarely used for high-dimensional FS problems due to their lack of noise.

P008_S0008.quote: minimizes the linear importance of a single FM while maximizing the geometrical similarity among distinct FMs
P008_S0008.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the QA maximizes the linear importance term and minimizes geometrical similarity, which is the exact opposite of what the perturbed text claims.
P008_S0008.original:
In this work, we propose a quantum annealing (QA)-based FS approach for generating the bit-strings containing the selected FMs:  the explanation maps of CNNs. 
By formulating FS as a QUBO problem, we leverage QA to efficiently identify the subset of FMs that most strongly contribute to a given prediction. 
The novelty we introduce relies in the fact that we do not apply FS directly on input pixels. Rather, we consider the hidden representations that the model learns at a particular stage as the elements over which performing the selection. Practically speaking,
we enforce the QA to sample the bit-string that maximizes the linear term representing the importance of a single FM to the prediction, while minimizing the geometrical similarity among distinct FMs through the cosine similarity term, in a way similar to the well-known NP hard Maximum Diversity problem \cite{kuo1993analyzing, ghosh1996computational, marti2013heuristics}.
In this way, we combine the core aspect of GradCAM (gradient importance) with a proper quadratic term (that considers the mutual geometrical similarity) to construct the QUBO problem.
P008_S0008.perturbed: In this work, we propose a quantum annealing (QA)-based FS approach. We enforce the QA to sample the bit-string that minimizes the linear importance of a single FM while maximizing the geometrical similarity among distinct FMs through the cosine similarity term, to ensure redundant representations are preserved.

P009_S0009.quote: increase scalability and dimensionality challenges...isolating FMs that have no contribution
P009_S0009.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states FS 'mitigates' (not increases) scalability challenges and isolates FMs that 'strongly contribute' (not have no contribution) to predictions.
P009_S0009.original:
Reducing the number of FMs by FS schemes provides a natural strategy to mitigate scalability and dimensionality challenges in DL models. Additionally, it improves model interpretation by isolating FMs that strongly contribute to a given prediction, compressing and discarding similar and redundant representations.
In this way we characterize the most informative patterns that the model learns during the training process to be able to interpret the resulting predictions.
P009_S0009.perturbed: Reducing the number of FMs by FS schemes provides a natural strategy to increase scalability and dimensionality challenges in DL models. Additionally, it improves model interpretation by isolating FMs that have no contribution to a given prediction, ensuring the most redundant patterns are learned.

P000_S0010.quote: the maximum energy gap between the first excited state and the ground state
P000_S0010.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'the minimum energy gap between the first excited state and the ground state', directly contradicting the perturbed 'maximum energy gap'.
P000_S0010.original:
The article is organized as follows: in sec. \ref{sec:results} we give a detailed description of the FS algorithm, defining the mathematical formulations and the QA simulation procedure. Subsequently, we present the main results, i.e. the explanation maps sampled by QA and the class-class correlation maps that quantify the disentanglement among distinct image classes. Then, we analyze the model's success by defining physics-derived metrics, such as the minimum energy gap between the first excited state and the ground state of the QUBO Hamiltonian and we explain the meaning behind these results.
In the sec. \ref{sec:discussion}, we discuss the relevance of our approach by explaining the key ideas behind the application of QA to the framework of interpretability, while highlighting our contributions, the novelty with respect to the state of the art, and the possible future directions that can be further explored.
P000_S0010.perturbed: The article is organized as follows: in sec. \ref{sec:results} we give a detailed description of the FS algorithm, defining the mathematical formulations and the QA simulation procedure. Subsequently, we present the main results, i.e. the explanation maps sampled by QA and the class-class correlation maps that quantify the disentanglement among distinct image classes. Then, we analyze the model's success by defining physics-derived metrics, such as the maximum energy gap between the first excited state and the ground state of the QUBO Hamiltonian and we explain the meaning behind these results.

P001_S0011.quote: searching through a small solution space that scales linearly with N_f
P001_S0011.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the solution space 'scales ~2^{N_f}' (exponentially), directly contradicting the perturbed claim of linear scaling.
P001_S0011.perturbed:
\section{Discussion}\label{sec:discussion}
DL architectures such as ResNet-18 can produce hundreds of FMs at a single convolutional layer. Identifying the most informative and non-redundant subset for a given prediction requires searching through a small solution space that scales linearly with $\mathbf{N_f}$, and classical FS algorithms are therefore the most efficient choice for these conditions. This fact motivates the use of quantum computers, in particular QA, to verify these classical results.

P002_S0012.quote: by directly modeling the architecture itself instead of focusing on the learned set of representations
P002_S0012.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the approach involves 'directly modeling the learned set of representations instead of focusing on the architecture itself', which is the reverse of the perturbed claim.
P002_S0012.original:
Our work introduces a novel approach to assess interpretability of large models, such as CNNs, by characterizing the learned FMs extracted from a target convolutional layer, and efficiently selecting those that contribute to uproot class-specific information from the data samples.
Our approach is motivated by the desire of performing a characterization of the entire set of FMs that compose the image. Each of them is appropriately inspected, revealing for which image class it is activated and whether it is shared among distinct images. We also achieve a significant model simplification, incrementing the level of confidence in interpreting predictive outcomes, by directly modeling the learned set of representations instead of focusing on the architecture itself.
To do so, we apply a FS algorithm to perform FMs characterization. Subsequently, we reformulate the FS algorithm as an energy minimization problem to be solved by a QA.
P002_S0012.perturbed: Our work introduces a novel approach to assess interpretability of large models, such as CNNs, by characterizing the learned FMs extracted from a target convolutional layer, and efficiently selecting those that contribute to uproot class-specific information from the data samples. Our approach is motivated by the desire of performing a characterization of the entire set of FMs that compose the image. Each of them is appropriately inspected, revealing for which image class it is activated and whether it is shared among distinct images. We also achieve a significant model simplification, incrementing the level of confidence in interpreting predictive outcomes, by directly modeling the architecture itself instead of focusing on the learned set of representations.

P003_S0013.quote: while obtaining a perfectly diagonal correlation matrix, our model ensures that representations are never shared among distinct image classes
P003_S0013.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the model 'also learns representations that are commonly found in distinct image classes' with non-zero overlap, directly contradicting the perturbed claim of perfect disentanglement.
P003_S0013.original:
As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:CC-corr}, while obtaining a quite disentangled correlation matrix, our model also learns representations that are commonly found in distinct image classes. For instance, classes representing animals may show a non-zero overlap since many of them are characterized by the same number of legs, similar color patterns or similar overall shape. Similarly, airplane images may be confused as ships or trucks because the overall shape of singular structures present in both classes is similar. 
In addition, we are assuming all the FMs that are selected belong to the subject of the classification. Rather, it may happen that a positive contribution to the gradient is obtained from a FM that is part of the background, and its selection is enforced through the quadratic term (it may be a geometrically independent vector with respect to those that contain information of the image subject). In this case, we can have distinct background-related FMs that are shared and activated within distinct image classes.  
Nevertheless, the feature characterization serves at this scope: understanding which filters are activated among distinct classes to further drive a more sophisticated training process that would take this into account.
P003_S0013.perturbed: As shown in Fig.~\ref{fig:CC-corr}, while obtaining a perfectly diagonal correlation matrix, our model ensures that representations are never shared among distinct image classes. For instance, classes representing animals show zero overlap since they are characterized by unique features. Similarly, airplane images are never confused as ships or trucks because the overall shape of singular structures is entirely distinct. In addition, we are assuming all the FMs that are selected belong to the subject of the classification.

P004_S0014.quote: achieves a higher Average Drop than full GradCAM and GradCAM++, confirming that QUBO-driven redundancy reduction yields less discriminative feature representations
P004_S0014.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the quantum FS 'achieves a lower Average Drop' and 'yields more discriminative feature representations', directly contradicting the perturbed claims.
P004_S0014.original: To benchmark the interpretability of the selected FMs, we evaluate the Average Drop $\%$ of the resulting CAMs. As shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:performance}, the quantum FS with $\mathbf{N_f=16}$ features achieves a lower Average Drop than full GradCAM and GradCAM++, confirming that QUBO-driven redundancy reduction yields more discriminative feature representations. Nevertheless, the Average Drop $\%$ is sensitive to the number of selected FMs and thus to the interplay parameter $\beta$ in the QUBO formulation from Eq.~\eqref{eq:QUBO_hamiltonian}, as it is observed in the results obtained in the $\mathbf{N_f}=512$ model, also presented in Tab.~\ref{tab:performance}.
P004_S0014.perturbed: To benchmark the interpretability of the selected FMs, we evaluate the Average Drop $\%$ of the resulting CAMs. As shown in Tab.~\ref{tab:performance}, the quantum FS with $\mathbf{N_f=16}$ features achieves a higher Average Drop than full GradCAM and GradCAM++, confirming that QUBO-driven redundancy reduction yields less discriminative feature representations. Nevertheless, the Average Drop $\%$ is insensitive to the number of selected FMs and the interplay parameter $\beta$.

P005_S0015.quote: SA successfully recovers a FM subset that perfectly matches the GradCAM and GradCAM++ class activation maps
P005_S0015.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'SA fails to recover a FM subset that faithfully represents the GradCAM and GradCAM++ class activation maps', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of success.
P005_S0015.original:
For the targeted convolutional block of the full ResNet-18, SA fails to recover a FM subset that faithfully represents the GradCAM and GradCAM$++$ class activation maps. Two compounding factors explain this result. First, verifying whether the SA solution reaches the global minimum of the QUBO problem is infeasible at $\mathbf{N_f}=512$, since neither simulated QA nor brute-force search scale to this problem size. Second, the spatial resolution of the FMs extracted from the last convolutional block of the full ResNet-18 is lower than in the custom architecture ($\mathbf{N_f}=16$), leading to larger pairwise cosine similarities $J_{pq}$ and a more aggressive selection fore the same $\beta$. As a result, the quadratic redundancy term in $\hat{H}_\mathrm{QUBO}$ suppresses a larger fraction of positively weighted FMs, leaving only a small surviving subset.
For a fixed $\beta$, this produces explanation maps with degraded Average Drop~\%. This case study underscores the importance of tuning $\beta$ until the Average Drop $\%$ converges to that of the reference CAMs. Furthermore, introducing a cardinality penalty constraining the number of selected FMs while leaving the redundancy term unaffected could improve the resulting explanation maps, at the cost of a bias toward a fixed number of selected features.
P005_S0015.perturbed: For the targeted convolutional block of the full ResNet-18, SA successfully recovers a FM subset that perfectly matches the GradCAM and GradCAM$++$ class activation maps. This is because verifying the SA solution reaches the global minimum is trivial at $\mathbf{N_f}=512$, as brute-force search scales linearly to this problem size. Second, the spatial resolution of the FMs extracted from the last convolutional block is higher than in the custom architecture, leading to smaller pairwise cosine similarities.

P006_S0016.quote: The distribution is well approximated by a Delta function at 1
P006_S0016.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'The distribution is well approximated by a Gaussian', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of a Delta function at 1.
P006_S0016.original:
In terms of model's complexity, we analyze in \ref{sec:methods} the couplings $J_{pq}$. The distribution is well approximated by a Gaussian, except for an initial bin peaked in correspondence of 0 value of $J_{pq}$ that coincides with the main diagonal of the cosine similarity matrix, which is 0 accordingly. The fact that the distribution is unstructured and represents a Gaussian means that our Ising model from the QUBO formulation falls into the well-known Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model \cite{das2005quantum, panchenko2013sherrington}, which, in its hardest instance is NP-Hard \cite{Barahona_1982}. 
This result is consistent with the well-known averaging effect in deep networks: as the number of FMs grows, individual feature representations become increasingly correlated and their pairwise overlaps self-average, suppressing large fluctuations. As a result, the effective QUBO instance is far from the hardest SK configurations, yielding a tractable optimization landscape.
P006_S0016.perturbed: In terms of model's complexity, we analyze in \ref{sec:methods} the couplings $J_{pq}$. The distribution is well approximated by a Delta function at 1. The fact that the distribution is structured means that our Ising model from the QUBO formulation avoids the Sherrington-Kirkpatrick model, which makes the optimization problem trivial and solvable in polynomial time. This result is consistent with the lack of correlation in deep networks.

P007_S0017.quote: The choice of including a mandatory constraining term that fixes the number of FMs to exactly 5
P007_S0017.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states 'The choice of not including a constraining term', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of including a mandatory constraining term fixing FMs to exactly 5.
P007_S0017.original: Tuning $\beta$ parameter may increase the complexity of the problem if combined with a more structured $J_{pq}$ distribution. In fact, other datasets can present more sophisticated coupling distributions that could not be estimated in a classical way. The choice of not including a constraining term that tunes how many FMs are part of the final solution bit-string simplifies the overall problem formulation, but this can be easily extended to include such a penalty term.
P007_S0017.perturbed: Tuning $\beta$ parameter decreases the complexity of the problem by removing the need for quantum annealing. In fact, other datasets present simple coupling distributions that are always estimated classically. The choice of including a mandatory constraining term that fixes the number of FMs to exactly 5 simplifies the overall problem formulation and is a requirement for the QA to converge.

P008_S0018.quote: applying the proposed methodology to earlier convolutional blocks significantly degrades the activation of FMs
P008_S0018.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the methodology 'can still improve the activation of those FMs that significantly contribute to the final prediction', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of degradation.
P008_S0018.original:
As we can see from Table \ref{tab:performance}, applying the proposed methodology to earlier convolutional blocks, where FMs do not yet exhibit a clearly interpretable semantic structure, can still improve the activation of those FMs that significantly contribute to the final prediction. 
A possible explanation is that performing FS at these early stages effectively acts as a structured information filtering mechanism. By suppressing redundant FMs before higher-level representations are formed, the CNN is encouraged to propagate only the most relevant low-level features. In this sense, the selection process can be interpreted as a form of information transfer: informative components that might otherwise be discarded by subsequent layers are preserved and transferred throughout the forward pass.
This effect becomes particularly evident in the deeper convolutional blocks and in the classifier, where the representations appear more focused and discriminative. Therefore, early-stage FM selection may improve the overall efficiency of representation learning by reducing redundancy and reinforcing task-relevant features across the network hierarchy.
P008_S0018.perturbed: As we can see from Table \ref{tab:performance}, applying the proposed methodology to earlier convolutional blocks significantly degrades the activation of FMs, as these layers contain no relevant information for the final prediction. A possible explanation is that performing FS at these early stages acts as a noise injection mechanism. By suppressing unique FMs, the CNN is discouraged from propagating any low-level features, ensuring that only the final classifier contains discriminative information.

P009_S0019.quote: it is restricted solely to ResNet-18 and cannot be extended to other models
P009_S0019.reason: The surrounding_context explicitly states the approach 'is flexible and can be extended to a wide range of alternative unsupervised and generative learning models', directly contradicting the perturbed claim of restriction to ResNet-18.
P009_S0019.original: Although our approach is model-dependent, meaning results can vary with different model structures or training setups, it is flexible and can be extended to a wide range of alternative unsupervised and generative learning models. As explained above, our work is universal: the FMs selection procedure can be sequentially applied to any intermediate convolutional block to study which trainable filters are activated across the whole network. We can also use our approach to reverse-engineer a FM search by penalizing the selection of orthogonal FMs and thus restricting our model to find those that are maximally aggregated. In conclusion, our novel approach to address the FS problem in DL and the QA mechanism that can deal with large optimization landscapes is flexible enough to be extended as an agnostic tool for explainable AI.
P009_S0019.perturbed: Although our approach is model-dependent, it is restricted solely to ResNet-18 and cannot be extended to other models. As explained above, our work is specific: the FMs selection procedure can only be applied to the final layer. We also find that our approach is incapable of reverse-engineering a FM search. In conclusion, our approach is a rigid tool that only works for specific image classification tasks and cannot be considered an agnostic tool for explainable AI.
